title: Banana bread with walnuts
subtitle: One-bowl quick loaf using very ripe bananas, with walnuts for crunch.
excerpt: Banana bread is a quick loaf that uses overripe bananas as the primary sweetener and moisture. Three black-spotted bananas, one bowl, one tin, and about an hour in the oven. The walnuts add texture and a slight bitterness that balances the sweetness. This loaf is better on day two than day one.
sourceNotes: Banana bread as a quick loaf became popular in the United States during the 1930s and appeared in homemaking manuals of that period, including early editions of 'The Joy of Cooking'. The chemical leavening (bicarbonate of soda) that makes it rise without yeast had become widely available in the previous decade. British versions became common post-war. This recipe follows the standard American quick-bread method: melt fat, mash fruit, combine wet and dry separately, fold together.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, then slice and freeze flat. Wrap each slice or stack with baking paper between. Defrost at room temperature or toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to three days. The flavour deepens on day two.

recipeTools.notes: Standard 2 lb (900 g) loaf tin, greased and lined with baking paper.

content.text: Banana bread needs very ripe bananas: black-spotted and soft, the ones you would not eat raw. The riper they are, the sweeter and more aromatic the loaf. Three medium bananas give about 280 g when mashed, which is enough to flavour and moisten the whole loaf without extra liquid.
This is a one-bowl recipe. The rule, as with all quick breads, is not to overmix once the flour goes in. A few strokes past smooth and the gluten tightens, making the loaf tough.

items.prepNote: very ripe, black-spotted, about 280 g mashed
melted and cooled to room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line it with baking paper, leaving enough paper to overhang the long sides.
Peel {{banana}} and mash them in a large bowl with a fork until mostly smooth. A few lumps add texture.
Add {{unsalted-butter}}, {{eggs}}, and {{light-brown-sugar}} to the bowl. Stir in {{vanilla-extract}} until just combined.
Sift in {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Fold with a spatula until the flour just disappears; about 12 strokes. Add {{walnuts}} and fold in with 3 or 4 more strokes.

Scrape into the prepared tin. Bake for 55 to 65 minutes. The loaf is done when a skewer comes out with moist crumbs but no wet batter, and the surface is deeply brown. Tent with foil if the top is browning too quickly after 40 minutes.
Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then lift out onto a rack using the paper. Leave for 30 minutes before slicing. The loaf is better the next day; wrap it in the paper and store in a tin.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Banana bread has few variables, but each one matters.

items.symptom: Loaf pale and flat after an hour.
items.cause: Bicarbonate of soda has lost its potency.
items.fix: Test a teaspoon in hot water before using: it should foam immediately. Replace any tin that is more than a year old.

items.symptom: Loaf wet and dense in the centre even after 65 minutes.
items.cause: Bananas too heavy, or oven running cool.
items.fix: Weigh the mashed banana; over 300 g slows the bake considerably. Check your oven temperature with an oven thermometer.

items.cause: Batter too liquid, or pieces too large.
items.fix: Break the walnuts into smaller pieces (no larger than a blueberry) and toss them in a little flour before adding. This helps them stay suspended in the batter.

content.text: Swap the walnuts for chocolate chips or pecans. Add half a teaspoon of ground cinnamon to the dry ingredients for a warmer flavour. A tablespoon of cocoa powder folded in with the flour turns it into a chocolate-banana loaf that is better than it sounds.
